subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the velocity of the particle at the mean position is given by

V=Aω   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

10=4×ω⇒ω=52 rad s-1

The formula to calculate the velocity of the particle at any instantaneous position x can be expressed as

v=ωA2-x2   ...2

From equation (2), it follows that

5=5242-x2⇒x2=16-4=12⇒x=12 cm

Hence, α=12.

Now, time period of spring-block system is given by,

T=2πmKeq=2πm2K

So, T=2π0.492×2

=2π49400=2π720=7π10

Now, number of oscillation in 14π is

N=timeT=14π7π10=20

subject: Physics
solution:
The amplitude of damped oscillation with time t and damping constant b is given by,

A=A0e-bt/2m

⇒lnA0A=bt2m

⇒ln2=b2m×120; here t=2 min=120 s

⇒0.693×2×1120=b

⇒b=1.16×10-2 kg s-1.

subject: Physics
solution:
By Newton's second law,

F=mamax.

The maximum acceleration for a SHM is given by 

amax=ω2A   ...(i)

The time period is given by 

T=2πω=3.14 s

⇒ω=2πT=2π3.14=2 rad s-1

Thus, the maximum acceleration from equation (i)

amax=4×1=4 m s-2

The maximum force is 

F=5 kg×4 m s-2=20 N

ω=2km=2×202

=20 rad s-1

Now, T=2πω=2π20=π5

Hence, required value of X=5.

solution:

Keq=K1K2K1+K2=K×2KK+2K=2K3

Therefore, the time period

Ta=2πmKeq=2πm2K3=2π3m2K

In figure (b), the springs are connected in parallel. Therefore,

Keq=K1+K2=3K

Therefore, the time period

Tb=2πmKeq=2πm3K

Now,

TaTb=92⇒Tb=23Ta=2 s

Hence, x=2

subject: Physics
solution:
For A: $y = \sin^2 \omega t = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\omega t}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{2} \cos 2\omega t$

This represents simple harmonic motion (SHM) about the mean position $y = \dfrac{1}{2}$ with an angular frequency of $2\omega$. The time period is $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{2\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$. Thus, A matches III.

For B: $y = \sin^3(2\omega t) = \dfrac{3\sin 2\omega t - \sin 6\omega t}{4}$

This is a superposition of two SHMs with angular frequencies $2\omega$ and $6\omega$. It is periodic but not SHM. The time period is the LCM of $T_1 = \dfrac{2\pi}{2\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$ and $T_2 = \dfrac{2\pi}{6\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\omega}$, which is $T = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$. Thus, B matches I.

For C: $y = \sin(\omega t) + \cos(\pi \omega t)$

The angular frequencies are $\omega_1 = \omega$ and $\omega_2 = \pi \omega$. The ratio $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}$ is an irrational number. Therefore, the function is non-periodic. Thus, C matches IV.

For D: $y = \cos \omega t + \cos 2\omega t$

This is a superposition of two SHMs with angular frequencies $\omega$ and $2\omega$. It is periodic but not SHM. The time period is the LCM of $T_1 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ and $T_2 = \dfrac{2\pi}{2\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$, which is $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$. Thus, D matches II.

The correct matching is A-III, B-I, C-IV, D-II.

Answer: A-III, B-I, C-IV, D-II

subject: Physics
solution:
Time period of oscillation is given by T=2πmk, where k is the force constant of the spring. 

∵ Spring is same, therefore, spring constant will be same.

Putting the different values,

In the first case          T=1=2πmk

In the second case    T'=2=2πm+3k

 Dividing the equations, we get

 TT'=12=mm+3

⇒mm+3=14⇒4m=m+3⇒m=1

Thus, m=1 kg

subject: Physics
solution:
(A) As both blocks moving together so Time period $=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{~K}}} ;$ where $\mathrm{m}=\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}$
$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{~K}}}$
(B) Let block is displaced by x in $(+\mathrm{ve})$ direction so force on block will be in(-ve) direction
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{Kx} \\ & (\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}) \mathrm{a}=-\mathrm{Kx} \\ & \mathrm{a}=-\frac{\mathrm{Kx}}{(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m})}
\end{aligned}$
(C) As upper block is moving due to friction thus
$\mathrm{f}=\mathrm{ma}=\frac{\mathrm{mKx}}{(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m})}$
(D) This option is like two block problem in friction for maximum amplitude, force on block is also maximum, for which both blocks are moving together.

$\begin{aligned}
& K A=(M+m) a \\ & a=\frac{K A}{(M+m)} \\ & f=m a=\frac{m K A}{(M+m)} \\ & f_{\max }=f_L=\mu \mathrm{mg} \\ & f=\mu m g \\ & \frac{m K A}{(M+m)}=\mu m g \\ & A=\frac{\mu(M+m) g}{K}
\end{aligned}$
(E) Maximum friction can be $\mu \mathrm{mg}$ as force is acting between blocks \& normal force here is mg.

subject: Physics
solution:
At equilibrium, the restoring force of the spring balances the weight of the mass:

$mg = kx_0$

$k = \dfrac{mg}{x_0} = \dfrac{0.2 \times 10}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000$ N/m

The frequency of oscillation is given by:

$f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$

$f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{1000}{0.2}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{5000} = \dfrac{10\sqrt{50}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{50}}{\pi}$ Hz

The maximum energy in the spring corresponds to its maximum elastic potential energy, which occurs at the maximum extension.

Maximum extension $x_{max} = x_0 + A = 2 \text{ mm} + 2 \text{ mm} = 4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m

Maximum energy in the spring $U_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} k x_{max}^2$

$U_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^2$

$U_{max} = 500 \times 16 \times 10^{-6} = 8000 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ J

Answer: $\dfrac{5\sqrt{50}}{\pi}$ and $8 \times 10^{-3}$

subject: Physics
solution: The formula of kinetic energy in S.H.M. is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mfenced><msqrt><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is the amplitude of the S.H.M. the formula of potential energy is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, so the net energy is defined as, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> so the total energy is always constant. Since, energy changes from potential to kinetic and vice-versa the average <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></math> in one time period is equal to the average <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></math> in one time period.

solution:
The formula to calculate the kinetic energy K of a particle executing SHM is given by

K=12kA2-y2   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the value of the unknown parameter.

K=12×k×A2-A2cos230°= 12×k×A2sin230°⇒200=12×1.0×10x×0.42×122= 2×10x100⇒102=10x-2⇒x-2=2⇒x=4

subject: Physics
solution:
Acceleration of vehicle down the incline will be a=gsinα
In the frame of the vehicle, we have to apply pseudo force. The Force diagram is shown in figure.
Component of mg along the inclined (i.e. mgsinα) will balance the pseudo force. Therefore, geff=gcosα
               

Hence,

T=2πLgeff=2πLgcosα.

solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& g=\frac{G M}{R^2} \\
& g^{\prime}=\frac{G(4 M)}{(2 R)^2}=g
\end{aligned}$

A is correct, $R$ is correct ; but since $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\ell}{g}}$ doesn't depend on mass ; R doesn't explain A .
